fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on fibers

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ({ f-=b } \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is right-cloven over \mathord { \vartriangle } A:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ({ fx=b } \right ) \mathclose {} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ({ x, \phi } \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ({ fx=b } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ({ y, \psi } \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p: x= y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p: x= y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p: x= y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p: x= y } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Dependent path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We have seen in how to obtain principles for equational reasoning by imposing path object structures on types; in general, we will need an analogous tool for working with families of types B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} given a path object \mathcal {A}. Such a tool must account for heterogeneous identifications in B, i.e. identifications between elements of different fibers of B; in this section, we explore three possible variations on the concept of “dependent path object”:

fr:ol.fr:li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
left-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
right-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased right- and left-cloven representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} over a reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for each x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} the component \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [{ x } \right ] \mathclose {} is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.
We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, \mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} is a path object.
Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. A left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} is given by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a pushforward operation assigning to each edge p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y a map p_* \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} y } \right \rvert \mathclose {};

a family \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ({ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x is the identity function.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. A right cleaving of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} is defined by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a pullback operation assigning to each edge p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y a map p^* \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} y } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {};

a family \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}^*b witnessing the unit law that pullback along the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x is the identity function.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord { \vartriangle } A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
We next show how right-cloven and left-cloven families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object. Any left-cloven family of path objects \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over \mathcal {A} in which:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y, we define b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ p } b' to be p_*b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} y } b';

for x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} and b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {}, the identity element \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} } {x} \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } b is given by the component \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} } xb \colon \mathopen {} \left ({ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } {b} of the unit law of the given right cleaving of \mathcal {B}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object. Any right-cloven family of path objects \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over \mathcal {A} in which:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y, we define b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ p } b' to be b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} y } p^*b';

for x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} and b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {}, the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {b} \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } b is given by the component \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} }xb \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}^*b of the unit law of the given cleaving of \mathcal {B}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of left cleavings (). Fixing a: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {}, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} with edges in b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b' and reflexivity data for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b is univalent.

It suffices to show that the type of singletons \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b' is a proposition. As each \mathcal {B} a is univalent, this is easily shown to be a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} a } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } b', which is a proposition again by the univalence of \mathcal {B} a. Therefore, we conclude via .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a left-cloven family of path objects over a path object \mathcal {A}. We define the covariant sum of the family \mathcal {B}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathcal {B} x}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to \mathcal {B} by . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ({ x,u } \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathcal {B} x} } \mathopen {} \left ({ y,v } \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} y } v.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an right-cloven family of path objects over a path object \mathcal {A}. We define the contravariant sum of the family \mathcal {B}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathcal {B} x}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to \mathcal {B} as in . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ({ x,u } \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx } \mathopen {} \left ({ y,v } \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } p^*v.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathcal {G} to \mathcal {G} ' is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ({ x \approx _{ \mathcal {G} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} \cong \mathopen {} \left ({ x \approx _{ \mathcal {G} ' } y } \right ) \mathclose {} that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ({ \lambda { x } \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, \mathopen {} \left ({ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {G} } {x} } \right ) \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} \sim \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {G} ' }.

We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ({ -xx } \right ) \mathclose {} is a left-cloven over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : \mathopen {} \left ({ \approx _{ \mathcal {G} } } \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} } \mathopen {} \left ({ \approx _{ \mathcal {G} ' } } \right ) \mathclose {} of graphs and a reflexivity datum r \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {G} } x, we define H_*r x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, \mathopen {} \left ({ rx } \right ) \mathclose {}; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ E: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ E: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ E: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ E: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x,y:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ({ Exx } \right ) \mathclose {}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on fibers

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ({ f-=b } \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is right-cloven over \mathord { \vartriangle } A:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ({ fx=b } \right ) \mathclose {} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ({ x, \phi } \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ({ fx=b } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ({ y, \psi } \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p: x= y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p: x= y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p: x= y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p: x= y } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ({ Hx } \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: As we evidently have \mathopen {} \left ({ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A } {s} } \right ) \mathclose {} ^*h x = \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . In this sense, sectionhood witnesses are right-cloven over their underlying maps.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f \colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon A \to B and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ B } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ B } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: As we have \mathopen {} \left ({ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A } r } \right ) \mathclose {} ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ A } over \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute are right-cloven over underlying maps.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on sections

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses form a right-cloven family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ({ s,h } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ({ s',h' } \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ({ \lambda { x } \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {act}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ({ H_s x } \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x } \right ) \mathclose {}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on retractions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B } \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses form a right-cloven family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ({ r,h } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ({ r',h' } \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ({ \lambda { x } \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ({ r x } \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x } \right ) \mathclose {}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an right-cloven family of path objects over a path object \mathcal {A}. We define the contravariant sum of the family \mathcal {B}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathcal {B} x}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to \mathcal {B} as in . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ({ x,u } \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx } \mathopen {} \left ({ y,v } \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y } \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } p^*v.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. A right cleaving of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} is defined by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a pullback operation assigning to each edge p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y a map p^* \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} y } \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {};

a family \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {B} x } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}^*b witnessing the unit law that pullback along the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x is the identity function.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Fiber

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The fiber of a function f \colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a \colon A equipped with identifications p \colon fa=b.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert { \mathcal {A} } \right \rvert \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ({ x, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } \right ) \mathclose {}. We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type A is a path object.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Embeddings are propositional

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any embedding f \colon A \to B is propositional.

We must check that for every b:B, the fiber of f at b is a proposition. Fixing u_0,u_1: \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, b we must check that u_0=u_1. We let \mathopen {} \left ({ a_0,p_0 } \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv u_0 and \mathopen {} \left ({ a_1,p_1 } \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv u_1 so that we have p_0 \colon fa_0=b and p_1 \colon fa_1=b. Invoking the path object structure on fibers (), it suffices to exhibit the following data:
As f is an embedding, the map \mathsf {act}_{ f } \colon a_0 = a_1 \to fa_0 = fa_1 has an inverse \mathopen {} \left ({ \mathsf {act}_{ f } ^{-1} , \epsilon , \eta , \chi } \right ) \mathclose {}.
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0 \cdot p_1 ^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0= \mathsf {act}_{ f } \phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \phi = p_0 \cdot p_1 ^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Propositional maps are embeddings

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any propositional map f \colon A \to B is an embedding.

Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{ f } \colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By and , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{ f } (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).

We first define the sections s_{x,y} \colon fx = fy \to x = y. Because f is assumed to be propositional, we have by an assignment \phi _{x,y}: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ h:fx=fy } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ h:fx=fy } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ h:fx=fy } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ h:fx=fy } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ({ x, \mathsf {refl} } \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ({ y,h ^{-1} } \right ) \mathclose {}. We therefore define s_{x,y}h : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ({ \phi _{x,y}h } \right ) \mathclose {} .1; the witness of sectionhood can be constructed using \mathopen {} \left ({ \phi _{x,y}h } \right ) \mathclose {} .2 modulo some identification algebra.

To construct the retractions r_{x,y} \colon fx=fy \to x = y, we note by our assumption that we have an assignment \psi _{x,y}: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ h:fx=fy } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ h:fx=fy } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ h:fx=fy } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ h:fx=fy } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ({ x, \mathsf {refl} } \right ) \mathclose {} =_{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ({ y,h ^{-1} } \right ) \mathclose {}, and we define r_{x,y}h : \equiv \mathsf {act}_{ -.1 } \mathopen {} \left ({ \psi _{x,y}h } \right ) \mathclose {}. It remains to show that for all x,y:A and h \colon x =y, we have \mathsf {act}_{ -.1 } \mathopen {} \left ({ \psi _{x,y}h } \right ) \mathclose {} =h. By induction on h it suffices to show that for all x:A we have \mathsf {act}_{ -.1 } \mathopen {} \left ({ \psi _{x,x} \mathsf {refl} } \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathsf {refl}. We will deduce that \psi _{x,x} \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} from our assumption that f \colon A \to B is propositional and thus fibred in sets via .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ({ g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } } \right ) \mathclose {} be a section of a function f \colon A \to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} P &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ({ g \mathopen {} \left ({ f x } \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ({ x, \mathsf {refl} } \right ) \mathclose {} \\ P & \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ x:A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : g \mathopen {} \left ({ f x } \right ) \mathclose {} = x } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : g \mathopen {} \left ({ f x } \right ) \mathclose {} = x } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : g \mathopen {} \left ({ f x } \right ) \mathclose {} = x } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ({ p : g \mathopen {} \left ({ f x } \right ) \mathclose {} = x } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {} \left ({ g \mathopen {} \left ({ f x } \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ({ x, \mathsf {refl} } \right ) \mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {} \left ({ g \mathopen {} \left ({ f x } \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} } \right ) \mathclose {} =_{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ({ fx } \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ({ x, \mathsf {refl} } \right ) \mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Invertible maps are contractible

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any invertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.

Let \epsilon \colon f \circ f ^{-1} = 1_{ B } be the counit of the inverse to f \colon A \to B. Given b:B, we must show that the fiber of f over b is contractible; the centre of contraction is given by the pair \mathopen {} \left ({ f ^{-1} x, \epsilon x } \right ) \mathclose {}, and the required paths can be constructed via the univalence principle for fibers using the unit \eta \colon f ^{-1} \circ f = 1_{ A }, the coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f, and the naturality of homotopies.
